title: Expected Value & Variance
description: The fair price of anything, and how much it wobbles. Uses the probability you just learned.

levels.title: The Fair Price
intro: Expected value is the probability-weighted average of outcomes: E[X] = Σ p·x. It's the 'fair price' of a random payoff — pay less and you profit on average.
Linearity is the superpower: E[X+Y] = E[X] + E[Y] always, even when X and Y are dependent.

exercises.prompt: A game pays you the face value of one fair die roll in dollars. What is the fair price of this game?
exercises.explanation: E[X] = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 3.5. Pay less than $3.50 and you make money on average; more and you lose.

exercises.prompt: A bet wins $10 with probability 0.3 and loses $4 with probability 0.7. Should a profit-maximizing quant take it (for free)?
options: No — you lose 70% of the time
exercises.explanation: EV = 0.3×10 − 0.7×4 = 3.0 − 2.8 = +$0.20. Losing often is fine if wins are big enough. Frequency of winning ≠ profitability.

exercises.prompt: A lottery ticket costs $1 and pays $500 with probability 1/1000. What is the EV of buying one ticket, in dollars? (negative if a loss)
exercises.explanation: E[payout] = 500/1000 = $0.50, minus the $1 cost → −$0.50. You surrender half your stake in expectation. Lotteries are a tax on ignoring EV.

exercises.prompt: True or false: E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] holds even when X and Y are dependent.
exercises.explanation: Linearity of expectation holds unconditionally. This is why hard-looking problems (like expected number of matches in a shuffled deck) collapse to easy sums.

exercises.prompt: Using linearity: what is the expected sum of two fair dice?
exercises.explanation: E[X+Y] = E[X] + E[Y] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7. No need to enumerate 36 outcomes — linearity did it in one line.

levels.title: Risk & Spread
intro: Two bets with the same EV can feel completely different. Variance measures the spread: Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])². Its square root is the standard deviation, in the same units as X.
Scaling rules: Var(cX) = c²·Var(X), and Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) only when X and Y are uncorrelated.

exercises.prompt: X is $0 or $10 with probability 1/2 each. What is Var(X)?
exercises.explanation: E[X] = 5, E[X²] = (0+100)/2 = 50, so Var = 50 − 25 = 25 (SD = $5). Every outcome is exactly $5 from the mean.

exercises.prompt: Bet A pays ±$1 on a fair coin; Bet B pays ±$10. Same EV (zero). Which statement is right?
options: B has 10× the variance of A
B has 100× the variance of A
Variance is undefined for fair bets
exercises.explanation: B = 10×A, and Var(cX) = c²Var(X), so variance scales by 10² = 100. Risk grows quadratically when you size up — a core trading fact.
Var(2X) = 2² × 3 = 12. Doubling position size quadruples variance — which is why position limits exist.

options: When X and Y are uncorrelated
exercises.explanation: In general Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2Cov(X,Y). The covariance term vanishes when they're uncorrelated. Correlation is what makes portfolio risk interesting.

exercises.prompt: What is the variance of a single fair die roll? (3 decimals ok)

levels.title: Games & Edges
intro: Now combine EV with waiting times. For a geometric process (repeat until success, probability p per trial), the expected number of trials is 1/p.
These 'expected time' puzzles are quant-interview classics — the trick is setting up an equation where the expectation refers to itself.

exercises.prompt: You get paid the SQUARE of a die roll in dollars. What is the fair price? (2 decimals)
exercises.explanation: E[X²] = 91/6 ≈ $15.17 — NOT 3.5² = 12.25. E[X²] ≠ (E[X])²; the gap between them is exactly the variance (2.92).

exercises.prompt: Rolling a fair die repeatedly, how many rolls do you expect to need to see your first 6?
exercises.explanation: Geometric with p = 1/6 → E = 1/p = 6. Elegant self-referential proof: E = 1 + (5/6)E, solve for E.

exercises.prompt: Expected number of fair-coin flips to see the pattern HH (two heads in a row)?
exercises.explanation: Let E = flips to HH. From scratch: E = 1 + ½E (tails, restart) + ½[1 + ½·0 + ½E] (head, then resolve). Solving gives E = 6. Fun fact: HT only takes 4 — patterns differ!

exercises.prompt: How many die rolls on average to see EVERY face at least once (coupon collector)?
exercises.explanation: Sum of geometric waits: 6×(1/6 + 1/5 + 1/4 + 1/3 + 1/2 + 1) = 6 × 2.45 = 14.7. Each new face is harder to find than the last.

exercises.prompt: Offer A: a sure $50. Offer B: a coin flip for $100 or $0. You'll face this choice 1,000 independent times. A risk-neutral quant thinks:
options: Equal EV; over 1,000 trials B's average converges near $50 anyway, so either is fine
exercises.explanation: Both have EV $50. Repetition shrinks B's per-trial risk (SD of the average falls like 1/√n), so for small repeated stakes EV is all that matters. For one huge bet, risk aversion kicks in — that's Unit 5.
